coredata.dc:title: Hybrid ultrasound-activated nanoparticles based on graphene quantum dots for cancer treatment
coredata.dc:creator: Ramedani, Arash
Sabzevari, Omid
Simchi, Abdolreza
coredata.dc:description: Theranostic liposomes have recently found a broad range of applications in nanomedicine due to stability, the high solubility of biomacromolecules, bioavailability, efficacy, and low adverse effects. However, the limitations of liposomes concerning the short systemic circulation in the body, limited controllability of the release rate, and the inability of in vivo imaging remain challenging. Herein, the development of novel hybrid ultrasound-activated piezoelectric nanoparticles based on a hybrid liposome nanocarrier composed of poly(vinylidene fluoride‐trifluoroethylene), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), and Silibinin (a hydrophobic drug) is presented. The hybrid nanoparticles are an acoustically sensitive drug delivery platform that releases the biomacromolecules in a specific tissue area (through surface labeling with PD-1 antibody) in a non-invasive and controlled manner. We show that the developed hybrid nanoparticles (with an average outer diameter of ∼ 230 ± 20 nm) enable piezoelectric-stimulated drug delivery combined with simultaneous fluorescent imaging of cancer cells in vivo. Significant enhancement (>80 % up to 240 h) and tunable drug release from the nanocarrier through enhanced diffusion from the liposome membrane are demonstrated. Cytotoxicity assays using MCF-7, 4T1, and NIH3T3 cell lines exhibit no confrontational influence of nanoparticles on cell viability up to 125 µg/ml. The PD-1 antibody on the surface of the hybrid nanocarrier allows for selective delivery to breast cancer tumors and low biodistribution to other tissues. Our results affirm that the developed ultrasound-activated piezoelectric nanoparticles have great potential as multifunctional platforms with sustainable release profiles for the delivery of hydrophobic drugs to breast cancer, especially when the ability for adequate labeling and cell monitoring is valued.

Structure and stability of the prepared nanoparticles. a) XRD patterns of nanoparticles. b) UV − Vis spectra of hybrid nanoparticles compared to Silibinin and piezoelectric nanoparticles. c) DLS graphs of nanoparticles at 25 °C and 37 °C. d) Stability of drug-loaded nanoparticles versus time at two different temperatures.
Ph-dependent release of silibinin from the hybrid nanoparticles at 37 °C.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Theranostic liposomes have recently found a broad range of applications in nanomedicine due to stability, the high solubility of biomacromolecules, bioavailability, efficacy, and low adverse effects. However, the limitations of liposomes concerning the short systemic circulation in the body, limited controllability of the release rate, and the inability of in vivo imaging remain challenging. Herein, the development of novel hybrid ultrasound-activated piezoelectric nanoparticles based on a hybrid liposome nanocarrier composed of poly(vinylidene fluoride‐trifluoroethylene), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), and Silibinin (a hydrophobic drug) is presented. The hybrid nanoparticles are an acoustically sensitive drug delivery platform that releases the biomacromolecules in a specific tissue area (through surface labeling with PD-1 antibody) in a non-invasive and controlled manner. We show that the developed hybrid nanoparticles (with an average outer diameter of ∼ 230 ± 20 nm) enable piezoelectric-stimulated drug delivery combined with simultaneous fluorescent imaging of cancer cells in vivo. Significant enhancement (>80 % up to 240 h) and tunable drug release from the nanocarrier through enhanced diffusion from the liposome membrane are demonstrated. Cytotoxicity assays using MCF-7, 4T1, and NIH3T3 cell lines exhibit no confrontational influence of nanoparticles on cell viability up to 125 µg/ml. The PD-1 antibody on the surface of the hybrid nanocarrier allows for selective delivery to breast cancer tumors and low biodistribution to other tissues. Our results affirm that the developed ultrasound-activated piezoelectric nanoparticles have great potential as multifunctional platforms with sustainable release profiles for the delivery of hydrophobic drugs to breast cancer, especially when the ability for adequate labeling and cell monitoring is valued.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past decade, one of the leading causes of global mortality with an estimation of > 10 million death has been a malignant tumor or malignant neoplasm known as cancer (Sung et al., 2021). One of the most challenging obstacles in cancer treatment is limiting the systemic toxicity effects of chemotherapeutic agents on the tumor environment, which contributes to the high mortality rates associated with cancer. Therefore, developing new approaches that selectively target cancer tissue seems essential (Hamblin, 2019; Mansoori et al., 2019). Nanomedicine based on customized nanoparticles have shown significant promise after several decades of scientific breakthroughs (Barjasteh et al., 2022).

Photodynamic therapy (PDT) is one of the most common methods in cancer treatment, combined with other forms of cancer therapy to improve treatment outcomes. In PDT, a chemical known as a photosensitizer (PS) kills cancer cells after being activated by light. The light may originate from a laser or any other source, such as light-emitting diodes (LEDs). This method boosts reactive oxygen species (ROS) levels, such as 1O2, within cells and their microenvironment (Agostinis et al., 2011). It is a non-invasive method and causes less destruction to surrounding tissues than other treatments. It also has fewer side effects due to its low toxicity. Studies have shown that ROS-responsive platforms, including PS, can destroy and kill cancer cells, usually with minimal damage to normal cells (Agostinis et al., 2011; Castano et al., 2006, 2004). Tetrapyrrole group (porphyrins, chlorins, bacteriochlorins, and phthalocyanine), synthetic dyes group (phenothiazinium, squaraine, and boron-dipyrromethene), and some natural molecular groups (such as hypericin, riboflavin, and curcumin) are examples of organic PS (Abrahamse and Hamblin, 2016). These systems not only enable us to treat cancer but also offer in vivo imaging simultaneously. The United States Food and Drug Administration has authorized some PS for pharmaceutical usage. However, these carriers have disadvantages, such as a lack of photostability, low solubility in water, and wavelength absorption range (O’Connor et al., 2009).

Recent studies have indicated that carbon nanostructures (CNs) are superior to PS concerning water dispersion and photostability(Han et al., 2019; Nie et al., 2020). Mainly, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are an auspicious material for PDT and imaging applications due to their photoluminescence (PL) properties (Chen et al., 2020; Fan et al., 2019; Ge et al., 2014; Kuo et al., 2020; Mangalath et al., 2021; Mushtaq et al., 2022; Santos et al., 2021; Tabish et al., 2018; Chong et al., 2016; Ge et al., 2014; Jovanović et al., 2015; Kuo et al., 2017; Markovic et al., 2012; Ristic et al., 2014; Thakur et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2018; Zhou et al., 2017). Ge et al. have demonstrated the potential of GQDs to generate singlet oxygen under blue, green, or even wide-spectrum light irradiation (such as xenon lamp spectrum). They have shown that GQDs can act as an efficient PS (through high singlet oxygen generation) and imaging agent for cancer cell monitoring (Ge et al., 2014). Despite these advantages, the biocompatibility of GQDs in clinical trials has been questioned (Hore et al., 2022; Maity et al., 2017; Sangam et al., 2022; Sapkota et al., 2017). As a result, numerous strategies, including surface modification of GQD with biocompatible polymers, have been used to reduce toxicity. For instance, Chandra et al.(Chandra et al., 2014) have shown that the agent’s toxicity could be mitigated by polyethylene glycol (PEG). Although PEG modification reduces cytotoxicity and ROS generation, intracellular penetration becomes more effective. In another study by Awad et al. (Awad et al., 2021), liposomes were employed to reduce the toxicity of GQDs. They have prepared liposome-encapsulated GQDs and triggered their release using ultrasound for delivering fluorescent probesto cancer cells. Their results have determined that GQDs can be delivered effectively to tumors with minimal toxicity by using low-frequency ultrasound (LFUS).

Combination therapy is a cornerstone of cancer treatment because it can overcome the challenges of tumor heterogeneity and drug resistance of cancer cells (Fan et al., 2017; Shrestha et al., 2019). Combining chemotherapy and PDT is an effective strategy that have recently attracted interest in clinical trials (Chen et al., 2019). For successful treatment, drug carriers should not be eliminated by the reticuloendothelial system (RES) to attain a high therapeutic index at the tumor (Abdel-kader, 2016; DeVita and Chu, 2008; Zimmermann et al., 2007). As a result, multifunctional drug delivery systems based on nanotechnology have emerged. To this end, liposomes have attracted much attention due to biocompatibility, portability of hydrophilic and hydrophobic drugs, good dispersion, ease of preparation, selectivity, physicochemical properties, and superior cellular uptake efficiency (An et al., 2017; Yoon et al., 2018, 2017; Zhang et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2018). The encapsulated drug must be released from liposomes before it can be absorbed by the surrounding cells in the tumor environment. Therefore, a rapid release is essential for fast-growing tumors (Drummond et al., 1999). Therefore, stimuli-responsive liposomes have been considered to improve therapeutic efficacy by increasing drug availability in the tumor. It is also possible to modify liposomes or individual constituents to have different surface structures for specific purposes, for example, targeting cells of tissues or imparting a “smart” character (Kim et al., 2019; Kou et al., 2020). Stimuli-responsive liposomes are very attractive for drug delivery because the macromolecules are released on demand when external or internal stimuli are presented (Lin et al., 2019). Several attempts over the last decade have been made to optimize local therapeutic delivery by stimulating drug release from liposomes. The hypothesis is that an environmental stimulus (such as enzymatic, hyperthermia, and pH) rearranges the structure of the liposome membranes and releases the encapsulated drug. Among different external techniques, ultrasound waves offer specific advantages for targeted drug delivery regardless of the depth of tissue (e.g., liver, brain, pancreas, and breast) (Ahmadi et al., 2020; Delaney et al., 2022; Lin et al., 2020; Walsh et al., 2021). Their membranes restrict the penetration of drugs into cells. Intensifying drug delivery within cells is challenging. However, employing ultrasonic waves is a promising strategy to overcome this problem. Ultrasonic waves penetrate soft tissues and induce stable cavitation, leading to increased cell membrane permeability. Although the thermal and chemical effects induced by ultrasonic waves are equally valuable for enhancing the permeability of the cell membrane, sonoporation is the primary mechanism by which drug permeability is enhanced in cancer cells (Awad et al., 2021; Sundaram et al., 2003). Liposomes, like cell membranes, are similarly susceptible to sonoporation. Consequently, drug release is controlled temporally and spatially (AlSawaftah et al., 2021; Awad et al., 2021, 2019; Ben Daya et al., 2021; Elamir et al., 2021; Salkho et al., 2018). Combining stimuli types, such as HIFU (high-intensity frequency ultrasound), radiofrequency, or microwave, with thermosensitive liposomes (TSL) in local hyperthermia of cancerous tumors increases the membrane permeability and thus the drug release kinetics. Drug release from TSL usually occurs at Tc and above (40–45 °C). However, one drawback of thermosensitive liposomes is that the entrapped therapeutics can be prematurely released into the blood circulation before reaching the cancer cells. In order to overcome this limitation, a non-thermal ultrasound strategy has been proposed to improve the efficiency of drug delivery to cancerous tumors. For this purpose, liposomes must be sensitized to ultrasonic waves by piezoelectric materials (Cotero et al., 2022; Lin et al., 2014, 2019; Marino et al., 2018; Rwei et al., 2017; Shalaby et al., 2020; Suzuki et al., 2010; Yin et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2020; Cotero et al., 2022).

On the other hand, some natural plant compounds have shown anticancer properties, which have substantially impacted cancer treatment (Tiwari and Mishra, 2015). For instance, Silibinin, a natural flavonoid, can cause apoptosis when it targets cellular signaling pathways, including mitochondrial apoptosis, ROS/receptor-mediated apoptosis, growth and transcription factors, angiogenesis, and metastasis. Thus, the drug presents a tremendous therapeutic potential for selective and efficient cancer treatment (Li et al., 2010; Singh et al., 2005). Previous studies have shown that Silibinin affects the production of ROS in MCF-7 breast cancer cells (Jiang et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2010a, 2010b; Zheng et al., 2017).

In this work, we have hypothesized that the hybridization of GQDs and Silibinin with an electroactive biopolymer addresses the cytotoxicity issues while enabling us with an ultrasound-stimulated drug delivery system. This study presents a novel nanocarrier for combination therapy based on dual stimuli-responsive liposomes enabling it to be activated by light and ultrasonic waves. The novelty of this work relies on the design of a hybrid ultrasound-activated piezoelectric nanoparticles enabling triggered and tunable release rate of natural-derived therapeutics. The nanoparticles have been designed based on piezoelectric macromolecules (P(VDF-TrFE)) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The concept is schematically shown in Fig. 1 . The combination of P(VDF-TrFE) nanoparticles and CQDs offers the capacity of dual-sensitivity (light and ultrasound) for better release rate control. The piezoelectric organic nanoparticles are conjugated with Silibinin, a well-known antioxidant material and antitumor agent (Binienda et al., 2020; Deep and Agarwal, 2010; Dheeraj et al., 2018; García-Maceira and Mateo, 2009). The hybrid nanoparticles, including Silibinin@P(VDF-TrFE) and GQD@P(VDF-TrFE), are encapsulated in liposomes by dehydration of the dry lipid film. We have hypothesized that the hybrid nanocarrier damages the membrane of cancer cells by generating ROS, and silibinin disrupts the function of the cell nucleus. The application of the dual-stimuli responsive nanocarrier with a high drug loading capacity and sustained release ability is demonetarized. The biocompatibility and biodistribution of the developed materials in the body are presented by in vitro and in vivo assays. The stimuli-responsive character of the hybrid nanocarrier and its tunability pave the way for new tactics to develop novel drugs based on piezoelectric nanostructures.

DSPC (1, 2-distearoyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphocholine), DOTA-NHS ester (1, 4, 7, 10-tetraazacyclododecane-1,4,7,10-tetraacetic acid mono-N-hydroxysuccinimide ester), DSPE (1,2-distearoyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphoethanolamine), Solvene®300P(VDF-TrFE), Silibinin, DPPC (1,2-dipalmitoyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphocholine), DAPI (4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole), Trypsin, EDTA solution, DMEM (Gibco Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium), p-PD (p-Phenylenediamine), DMSO (dimethyl sulfoxide), Diphenyl ether, Hexane, Ethanol, Cholesterol, Chloroform, NHS (N-hydroxysuccinimide), EDC (1-Ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride), MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide), and PBS solution (pH 7.4) were supplied by Sigma–Aldrich and Merck & Co., Inc. (USA). NIH3T3 mouse fibroblast cells, 4T1 breast mouse cancer cells, and MCF-7 human cancer cells were obtained from the Faculty of Pharmacy of Tehran University (Iran). The programmed death monoclonal antibody (PD-1 mAb) was purchased from Invitrogen (Thermo Fisher Scientific Corporation, USA).

A solution of p-phenylenediamine (0.2 g) in diphenyl ether (1 mL) was prepared at 80 °C. This solution was rapidly injected into a round-bottomed three-neck flask (15 mL) equipped with a reflux condensation apparatus heated at 200 °C. The mixture was stirred for 8 h. After cooling to room temperature, the suspension is poured into hexane (100 mL) to precipitate CQDs. The product was separated by centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 20 min. The product was washed three times via precipitation and centrifugation to achieve 0.164 g GQDs.

3 mg P(VDF-TrFE) was dissolved in acetone (10 mL) and vortexed at 30 rpm for 1 min. The polymer solution was then mixed with 1 mg GQDs. The mixture solution was injected with a syringe into a beaker containing water at a flow rate of 1 mL/min and simultaneously sonicated to attain GQD@P(VDF-TrFE) NPs. Three 5-minute ultrasonic cycles were carried out using a Sonics Vibra-cell (Sonics & Materials, Inc., USA) operating at 20 kHz. A similar procedure was utilized to prepare Silibinin@P(VDF-TrFE) NPs. The drug concentration in the polymer solution was 1 mg/mL.

PD-1 mAb-modified liposomes containing Silibinin@P(VDF-TrFE) and GQD@P(VDF-TrFE) were produced using the thin lipid film hydration technique (Ferreira et al., 2016). It is known that amino groups of PD-1 mAb interact with NHS groups of DSPE-PEG-NHS to form an amide bond (Du et al., 2017a). Therefore, PD-1 mAb was combined with DSPE-PEG-NHS at a 1:10 M ratio in an 80 percent DMSO solution (pH 8.5–9.0). The reaction was gently agitated for 2 h at 37 °C. A SEC3000 liquid chromatography was used to purify the finished product and separate the components. Using an Amicon centrifugal filter, the resulting solution (DSPE-PEG-PD-1) was concentrated and lyophilized.

A solution of DSPC, cholesterol, DSPE-DOTA, and DSPE-PEG-PD-1 (molar ratio: 49:44:2:2.5) in chloroform/methanol (4:1, v/v) was prepared. The organic solvent was removed in a rotary evaporator to form the lipid at 40 °C/50 rpm. After the formation of the lipid, the system was purged with nitrogen. The formed film was then saturated with a citrate-buffered solution (10 mL; pH = 4) in a bath sonicator for 10 min. The pH was then adjusted to 7 by dropwise addition of bicarbonate buffer (pH = 0.5). In the next step, Silibinin@P(VDF-TrFE) and GQD@P(VDF-TrFE) nanoparticles were mixed in the liposome solution of GQD@P(VDF-TrFE) NPs/Silibinin@P(VDF-TrFE) NPs/lipid (w/w) at a ratio of 1:1:10 and incubated for 24 h at room temperature. HPH was used to extrude the liposome mixture a total of ten times.

The solution was dialyzed against DI water for 24 h to remove the free drug from the solution. The obtained product was then freeze-dried at − 50 °C and 0.1 mbar for 48 h. The efficiency of drug loading (DL) and encapsulation (EE) was determined by UV–vis spectrometry (LAMBADA 35 spectrophotometer, PerkinElmer). Different ratios of drug to liposomes were examined, and the following equations were utilized(Song et al., 2019):

A Rayleigh UV-2601 spectrophotometer (Beijing Beifen-Ruili Analytical Instrument Co., ltd., China) was used to record UV–vis spectra. This analysis was carried out at 4 °C and 37 °C at different time intervals (up to 30 days) to assay the suspension stability. FT-IR spectrum was obtained by ABB Bomem MB100 FTIR spectrometer in transmission mode using KBr pellets. The spectral resolution was 4 cm−1. Dynamic light scattering (ZEN3600 Malvern) was used to determine the size distribution and zeta potential of the nanoparticles at 25 °C and 37 °C. High-resolution scanning electron microscopy (TESCAN–Mira3) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, 100 kV, Philips CM 120) were employed to investigate nanoparticle size, morphology, and structure. Fluorescence spectroscopy was carried out on an Ava-Spec-2048 TEC apparatus (Avantes, USA). An XploRA plus confocal spectrometer (Horiba ltd., Japan) was used to capture Raman spectra.

The quantum yield (QY) of GQDs, GQD@PVDF-TrFE NPs, and HNPs was evaluated according to a typical slope approach using Rhodamine B in ethanol as a reference sample (Kubin and Fletcher, 1982). It was calculated according to the following equation (Deng et al., 2019):

IRB and Iex are the slopes calculated from the curves (integrated PL intensity vs absorbance) of the Rhodamine B and experiment, respectively, and nRB and nex are the refractive indexes (1.36 for ethanol). In the same experiment solutions, nex/nRB is equal to 1.

2 mg/mL nanoparticles were inserted into a dialysis bag and placed in 200 mL PBS (pH 7.4) with Tween-80 (0.5 % w/w) at 37 °C under continuous stirring. The incubation media (3 mL) was removed and exchanged with PBS at predetermined intervals. UV–vis spectroscopy measured the quantity of the released drug in the incubation medium. The piezoelectric sensitivity of the nanoparticles was investigated using an ultrasonic probe at the following conditions: maximum acoustic pressure: 0.1 MPa; frequency: 1.5 MHz; signal repetition frequency: 1.007 kHz; temporal power: 300 mW/cm2. The release mechanism was evaluated by fitting experimental data into kinetic models.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cell viability and cellular uptake assay
ce:section.ce:para: For the MTT assay, 96-well plates were used to seed NIH3T3, 4T1, and MCF-7 cells. The culture medium was RPMI-1640 containing fetal bovine serum (10 % (v/v)), penicillin (40–50 U/mL), and streptomycin (50 U/mL). The cells (1000 cells in each well) were incubated at 37 °C in an incubator with 5 % CO2 for 24 h. Cell culture media included with the relevant materials (blank cells and hybrid nanoparticles at different concentrations (62.5, 125, 250, 500 µg/mL)) were used to replace the previous medium. 5 mg/mL MTT solution was applied to each well after 1 or 3 days of incubation. After incubating for 4 h, the medium was changed with dimethyl sulfoxide and shaken for 10 min. The optical density was measured using a microplate reader to determine cell viability at 570 nm.

Furthermore, Confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) and flow cytometry (FCM) were utilized to determine the cellular uptake of nanoparticles. Cellular uptake of the hybrid nanoparticles was studied in MCF-7 cell lines. The cells were seeded onto 12-well plates and incubated overnight. Then, 62.5 µg/mL NPs were added and incubated for 24 and 48 h. The uptake was observed by an Eclipse TE2000-S Nikon fluorescent microscope. Furthermore, nuclei were labeled with DAP. Cell visualization after 48 h incubation was carried out by a Leica TCS SPE confocal laser scanning microscope (Germany). The excitation wavelength was 488 nm. Also, for FCM analysis, 4T1 cell LINES were treated with trypsin and then centrifuged at 2000 rpm for 3 min. The fluorescence intensity of the cells that had been collected was then measured with a flow cytometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo biodistribution and toxicity evaluation
ce:section.ce:para: Female BALB/c mice (weight 23–25 g; 7–8 weeks old) were provided by the Pasteur Institute. Histopathological studies were performed by injecting different concentrations of NPs into the animal models. After 24 h intravenous (IV) injections into the lateral tail veins of BALB/c mice, the animals were euthanized. The harvested tissues, including the liver, heart, kidney, and lung, were preserved in 10 % NBF (pH 7.26) for 48 h before processing and embedding in paraffin. A cross-section (5 µm thick) was prepared and stained with hematoxylin and eosin. An Olympus BX51 light microscopy was used to examine the histology slides. Every alteration, from acute and chronic inflammation to fatty changes to bleeding and coagulation necrosis, was evaluated in various samples.

The Pasteur Institute provided female BALB/c mice bearing cancerous tumors. Tumor growth became visible 2–3 weeks after injection of approximately 2 × 103 4T1 cells. The shaved mice were anesthetized with 30–40 µL xylazine (5 mg/kg)/ ketamine (100 mg/kg). Then, the tumors were observed under a fluorescence microscope (FluoVision, 16MP CCD camera, developed in TUMS Preclinical Core Faculty) equipped with excitation (390–630 nm) and emission (450–800 nm) filters. Afterward, dispersed nanoparticles in PBS (pH = 7.4) were injected into the mice (5 mg/kg) via the tail vein. The fluorescence signals were recorded at appropriate times.

Each experiment was carried out in triplicate. The outcomes were reported as the mean value ± standard deviation (SD). A one-way multifactorial analysis of variance was carried out for comparison across different groups. A p-value less than 0.05 was considered statistically significant.
All studies were carried out in agreement with the protocols set by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of Tehran University of Medical Sciences (TUMS).

Spectroscopic (FT-IR, Raman, UV–vis, and PL) and microscopic techniques (high-resolution SEM and TEM) were employed to investigate the characteristics of NPs. The FT-IR analysis was employed to determine functional groups and chemical bonds (Fig. 2
a). The spectrum of GQDs exhibits characteristic peaks at about 2848–2918 cm−1, 1336–1384 cm−1, and 1225–1234 cm−1, corresponding to the aliphatic stretching vibrations of CH, CN, and CO, respectively (Lin et al., 2017). These peaks do not appear in the starting material (p-PD) spectrum used to synthesize the carbon nanostructure, which implies the occurrence of intermolecular cyclization, decomposition, and condensation reactions during processing. The characteristic peaks at 510 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1 can be attributed to the starching vibration of CH2 and the bending of CF2 bands of P(VDF-TrFE), respectively (Arrigoni et al., 2020). The symmetric stretching of CF2 appears at 850 cm−1. Peaks of CH2 are observed at around 3027 cm−1 and 2985 cm−1 (Habibur et al., 2018). The spectrum of Silibinin consists of out-of-plane bending of OH (995 cm−1) and several stretching bands, including CC (1125 cm−1), CO (1160 cm−1 and 1180 cm−1), COC (1269 cm−1), aromatic alkene (1030 cm−1 and 1510 cm−1), CC and CO (1510 cm−1, 1610 cm−1, 1633 cm−1), OH (2960 cm−1), and –OH and = CH (3360 cm−1) (Ramedani et al., 2022; Pooja et al., 2014). The imide bond of DSPE-PEG-NHS is changed to the amide bond by reacting with the amine side group of the N-terminus PD-1 mAb. In addition, the formation of a stable amide bond by coupling reaction was confirmed by FT-IR analysis ( Figure S1 ). The spectra of DSPE-PEG-NHS and DSPE-PEG-PD-1 indicate that the 1713 cm−1 (imide bond) peak is shifted to 1628 cm−1 (amide bond) due to the coupling reaction (Park et al., 2021). In the formulation of synthesized composite and hybrid nanomaterials, the formation of new complexes is not noticeable.

The calculated quantum yields of GQDs, GQD@PVDF-TrFE NPs, and HNPs under 530 nm excitation in absolute ethanol are about 35 %, 27 %, and 23 %, respectively ( Figure S2 a-c). High QY can be attributed to the nitrogen-doped GQDs, which are good auxochromes and show a dramatic increase in the QY compared to non-nitrogen CDs (0.5–1.8 %) (Deng et al., 2019). The PL intensity of GQDs, GQD@PVDF-TrFE NPs, and HNPs under 360 nm excitation was assessed for 200 min to explore the fluorescence stability of the nanoparticles ( Figure S2 d). Although the PL intensity of HNPs is lower than GQDs and GQD@PVDF-TrFE NPs, the fluctuations in PL intensity throughout 120 min are practically constant for HNPs, demonstrating the excellent fluorescence stability of HNPs for biological applications.

Fig. 2 b shows the fluorescence spectrum of the nanoparticles in ethanol at the different excitation wavelengths. The nanoparticles exhibit a strong PL emission at around 600 nm with an excitation-dependent behavior, i.e., a slight redshift is observed when a higher excitation wavelength is used. It is known that the fluorescent intensity and emission wavelength of CQDs depend on the size, size distribution, and nitrogen content (Qu et al., 2012). Fig. 2 c shows the absorption spectrum as compared with p-PD. The absorption peaks at 286 nm and 512 nm could correspond to
π
- π
∗
a n d
n - π
transitions (Lin et al., 2017). The appearance of different electronic states is linked to the aromatic sp2 domains and functional groups with amine edges (Lin et al., 2017).

Fig. 2 d displays the Raman spectra of GQD, GQD@P(VDF-TrFE), Silibinin@ P(VDF-TrFE), and HNP nanoparticles. The β-phase bands for P(VDF-TrFE) can be observed at 840 cm−1 (CF2 symmetric stretching) (Ullah et al., 2019). In the case of Silibinin@P(VDF-TrFE) NPs, the collective symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of CC bonds that correspond to rings of the silibinin, as well as COH bending and CO stretching vibrations are the origins of the Raman active modes in the 1200–1600 cm−1 frequency domain (Solís-Gómez et al., 2019). The peaks in the areas of 787 cm−1, 1090 cm−1, 1450 cm−1, 1653 cm−1, 2850 cm−1, and 2940 cm−1 are respectively related to OPO symmetry stretch vibration, CC stretching (lipid), CH2 scissoring (lipid), CC stretching (lipid), CH2 stretching asymmetric (lipid) and CH3 stretching symmetric (lipid) (Solís-Gómez et al., 2019). Along with the HR-TEM image of GQDs (Figure 3S), the results of Raman spectroscopy (Fig. 2 d) and the appearance of d-band (1354 cm−1) and G-band (1584 cm−1) determine that GQDs are synthesized. The intensity ratio of D to G bands is almost equal (ID/IG = 0.95), indicating that the quantum dots have low structural defects. The ID/IG ratio increases from 0.95 (GQD) to 0.97)GQD@P(VDF-TrFE)(, and 0.99 (HNP). This finding reveals that the concentration of defects is reduced due to a strong interaction between GQDs and P(VDFTrFE). It has been hypothesized that the –CH groups in the P(VDF-TrFE) molecule chains and the –OH and –NH groups of the GQDs could combine to form polarized water molecules within the interlayers of the GQDs. It would lead to a reduction in the number of dangling bonds and defects in the GQDs.

Fig. 3
a and 3b show electron micrographs of the hybrid NPs. These nanoparticles, which result from self-assembly and lipid thin-film hydration, present a spherical morphology with an average particle size of 230 ± 20 nm. Fig. 3 c-3f show electron micrographs of P(VDF-TrFE) NPs. The particles have a spherical morphology with narrow size distribution with an average diameter of 40 ± 10 nm. The formation of the particles is proceeded based on the self-assembly of the constituents induced by robust hydrophobic interactions between fluorine groups of the macromolecules and hydrous in the aqueous solution (Xiao et al., 2013). It is known that non-polar solutes with hydrophobic interactions can disrupt hydrogen bonds between water molecules (Silverstein, 1998; Xiao et al., 2013). Therefore, the P(VDF-TrFE) chain tends to aggregate in the form of nanoparticles because it is incapable of hydrogen bonds with water molecules. It is worth mentioning that the size of NPs can be modified by adjusting the polarity of the solvent. For instance, the synthesis of P(VDF-TrFE) NPs in the presence of Silibinin and a water/methanol mixture (1:10 v/v) significantly reduces the average particle size by a factor of 10 (Fig. 3 c and 3d). A change in morphology from spherical to irregular shapes is also noticed. The presence of GQD in GQD@P(VDF-TrFE) NPs was confirmed by HRTEM image (Fig. 4
S) and the average diameter of these particles is 50 ± 10 nm (Fig. 3 e and 3f). XRD analysis determines that the P(VDF-TrFE) nanoparticles primarily consist of the β-phase (Bai and Ducharme, 2004; Guo et al., 2005) with intensive and sharp characteristic peaks of (110) and (220) (Fig. 4 a). The interplanar spacing for the (110) peak is 0.46 nm matching with the β-phase (Xiao et al., 2013). Based on the peak intensity ratio(Lu et al., 2016), the amount of β-phase is estimated to be ∼ 85 %. The addition of GQDs did not change the phase characteristics of the polymer remarkably. It is pertinent to point out that GQDs should exhibit a weak and broad XRD peak(Ahmed et al., 2018) at around 25°, but it overlaps with that of the polymer.

The Silibinin@P(VDF-TrFE) nanoparticles were loaded into the liposome by dehydration of the dry lipid film. From Fig. 2 a and 4b, it can be deduced that the loading of the nanoparticles in the nanoliposomes does not influence the prominent IR characteristic peaks. Therefore, it is proposed that drug encapsulation is carried out without significant chemical connections. Nevertheless, the intensity of the characteristic peak of Silibinin at the wavelength of 288 nm, which is related to the π-π* electron transition in aromatic groups (Romanucci et al., 2017), is amplified after encapsulation. This finding agrees with previous studies on Silibinin-loaded liposomes (Han et al., 2018; Patel et al., 2022), showing that conjugated drug with P(VDFTrFE) improves the encapsulation efficiency.

Table 1 reports the encapsulation efficiency (EE) and drug loading capacity (DL) at different drug/lipid ratios. The results determine that with increasing the ratio, both values of DL and EE are reduced. The presence of large hydrophobic blocks in the lipid and P(VDF-TrFE) should enhance entrapment efficiency; however, the drug is located in the hydrophobic core of P(VDF-TrFE) by non-covalent interactions, as reported in other drug delivery platforms (Ghaffari et al., 2018).

Ideally, the most effective drug should be encapsulated with the lowest amount of lipids (drug-to-lipid ratio, D:L) (Khan et al., 2008; Levy et al., 2021). EE% and DL% are directly related to the D:L ratio; as the D:L ratio decreases, the EE% and DL% also decrease. These results indicate that the amount of Silibinin has saturated the lipid bilayer, which leads to an increase in the amount of free Silibinin and drug migration to the aqueous phase.

DLS was used to determine the hydrodynamic size and size distribution of the prepared nanoparticles (Fig. 4 c). Drug loading was carried out at 25 °C and 37 °C. The results are shown in Table 2 . The differences in the size analysis between TEM and DLS arise from the method of measurements (dry and hydrated states) and the double-layer effect (Li et al., 2012; Nguyen et al., 2011). The results indicate that by increasing the synthesis temperature, finer particles are attained because the ratio of the bending elastic modulus of the membrane to the line tension is changed (Zook and Vreeland, 2010). Other studies have shown that other temperature-related parameters, such as free energy, viscosity, and diffusion coefficients, seem to have almost no impact on liposome size (Zook and Vreeland, 2010). Hydrophobic drugs are believed to be encapsulated in nanoliposomes physically, which occurs through secondary forces, including van der Waals, hydrophobic interactions, and hydrogen bonding (Ghaffari et al., 2018). Because Silibinin is insoluble in water, the drug loading efficiency in nanoparticles is lower when the drug is dispersed in a buffer solution and encapsulated in the liposome than when it is conjugated with the polymer and then encapsulated in the liposome.

Stability in aqueous solutions and the ability to release a pharmaceutical agent in response to an external stimulus in the biological environment are critical requirements for an efficient drug delivery system (Ghaffari et al., 2018). Therefore, we have studied the colloidal stability and drug retention of the synthesized nanoparticles at 4 °C and 37 °C in PBS. Our experiments have determined that no agglomeration and sedimentation occur during storage at both temperatures. It is known that colloidal stability is controlled by zeta potential (Zook and Vreeland, 2010). The zeta potential of the nanocarrier at 4 °C and 37 °C is −26 and −25.6 mV, respectively. The monoclonal antibody and the phenolic groups in Silibinin could be responsible for the nanoparticles' negative charge. Electrostatic and steric stabilization are responsible for the excellent stability of the nanocarrier. Fig. 4 d shows the percentages of drug retention at the examined temperatures. It is seen that only slight changes (up to ∼ 10 %) in the absorbance have happened versus time at 4 °C. Lower drug retention is attained at 37 °C, i.e., the retention is about 70 % after 14 days. The mechanism of drug release depends on many factors and is influenced by the degradation of the lipid, the interaction between the drug and carrier, and drug/polymer deformation speed (Lindner and Hossann, 2010). Therefore, the role of temperature in drug retention can be ascribed to the release kinetics and its mechanism, which are generally controlled by the permeability of the nanocarrier and drug diffusion coefficient.

The pH sensitivity of HNPs was demonstrated by the release curve of Silibinin from HNPs (Fig. 5
). During the drug release process, the proportion of the overall release ratealways shows a noticeable shift in response to different conditions. The drug release rate increases from 9.7 % (pH = 7.4) to 14.5 % (pH = 6.4) and 17.5 % (pH = 5.4) after 240 h. This indicates that a decrease in pH improves the release rate to about 80 %. This observation indicates that a decrease in the pH improves the release rate to about 80 %. Applying ultrasound waves increases the drug release rate, particularly at lower pH values. After 240 h, the amount of released drug is 80 % (pH = 7.4), 84 %(pH = 6.4), and 88 %(pH = 5.4). These results indicate that ultrasonic waves significantly increase the amount of drug released through the mechanical deformation of nanoparticles (sonoporation process). As a result, the effect of the surrounding environment's acidity is insignificant compared to the impact of ultrasound waves.

The amount of released drug from different nanocarriers versus time at 37 °C is shown in Fig. 6 . The effect of applying ultrasonic waves is also presented. Without sonification, the release rate is higher at the early stage (up to 46 h) and becomes progressively with a lower rate in the second phase controlled by diffusion (Feldman et al., 2018). Applying ultrasound waves significantly accelerates the release kinetics. The time of the initial stage is shifted to 48 h while the release rate in the diffusion-controlled phase is highly promoted. For instance, the amount of released drug from the hybrid nanocarrier is > 80 % after 240 h with sonication compared to about 9 % for the control (without sonication). It is also visible that the drug release rate from Silibinin@PVDF-TrFET and Silibinin@Liposome increases by about 600 % and 250 %, respectively, compared with the control (no sonification). The mechanism of accelerated release kinetics under ultrasonic waves is ascribed to the mechanical deformation of nanoparticles and internal cavitation of micro-and nano-bubbles (Su et al., 2021). With mechanical stresses and polarization/depolarization effects in the piezoelectric polymer matrix, the release of Silibinin is enhanced because nanoliposomes are easily ruptured when exposed to low-frequency ultrasonic waves (Lin and Thomas, 2004). Previous studies have determined that low-frequency ultrasonic waves do not change or disrupt the activity of the encapsulated drug when applied to liposomes (Schroeder et al., 2007). Meanwhile, the amount of released drug from Silibinin@ PVDF-TrFET after 240 h is more pronounced than the hybrid nanocarrier (91 % versus 80 %) in the HNP sample. This observation can be attributed to the liposome structure, as it can be broken by ultrasonic waves and rearranged in the aqueous medium by self-assembly, encapsulating some of the drugs.

The following kinetic models have been employed to fit the experimental data to determine nanoparticle drug-release processes (Ghaffari et al., 2018).

Where K, t, and n are the rate constant, time, and “orders” of the rate equation, respectively. The values of these parameters and the regression coefficient (R2) are summarized in Table 3
. The results indicate that the power-law equation (Eq.
(7) ) best fits the data. The findings also demonstrate that liposomes trigger a more controlled drug release. Moreover, the values of n indicate that diffusion (between 0.44 and 0.82) is the most likely dominant release mechanism from the nanoliposome, in agreement with previous studies (Ashrafizadeh et al., 2020; Ghalehkhondabi et al., 2021; Ripoli et al., 2016). It is possible to conclude that the release of Silibinin was non-Fickian, which exhibited diffusion and controlled drug release (Mircioiu et al., 2019; Pippa et al., 2014).

The biocompatibility of liposomal vehicles is critical for the delivery of drugs. After incubating the drug-loaded nanoparticles with 4T1, MCF-7, and NIH3T3 fibroblast cells, we performed an MTT assay. The viability of cells at different drug doses (0, 62.5, 125, 250, and 500 µg/ml), times (24, 48, and 72 h), and under external factors (light and ultrasound waves) is shown in Fig. 7 . The viability of the drug (Silibinin) and the control (without drug injection) are presented as a reference for comparison purposes. The characteristics of photodynamic therapy (PDT) and ultrasound sensitivity were investigated by applying external forces of a xenon lamp (light) and ultrasound waves, creating four possible modes in this experiment: the first is no external factor and in a dark environment (D); the second is the dark environment and the presence of ultrasound waves (D + US); the third is the light (L); the fourth category is the light and in the presence of ultrasound waves (L + US). The results indicate that the cell viability in the drug-free specimens is > 90 % even under external factors, determining the negligible influence of the factors (under the conditions used in this experiment) on the cell cycle. However, the addition of nanoparticles negatively influences the viability depending on the concentration (up to 500 µg/ml). Fig. 7 b illustrates the half-maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50) for different drug doses, times, external factors, and nanoparticles. The results indicate that IC50 in cancer cells (MCF-7 and 4T1) is lower than in fibroblasts (NIH3T3). The drug kills cancer cells by activating cell death pathways and differentiates between normal and cancer cells. For instance, if MCF-7 and 4T1, and NIH3T3 cell lines are incubated with Silibinin for 24 h in the dark, IC50 decreases from 250 µg/ml for NIH3T3 cells to 200 µg/ml for cancer cells, which means less damage to normal cells in the treatment process. Furthermore, there is a direct relationship between incubation time and IC50 due to cell growth and proliferation. For example, if the NIH3T3 cell line is incubated with Silibinin in the dark, the IC50 increases from 250 µg/ml to 400 µg/ml by increasing the incubation time from 24 to 72 h. Finally, external stimuli (such as light and ultrasound waves) reduce the IC50, indicating that the nanoparticles are responsive to external stimuli. For example, ultrasound waves reduce the IC50 from 110 µg/ml to 100 µg/ml; in the presence of light, IC50 reaches 95 µg/ml; and if both factors are applied equally, this value decreases to 88 µg/ml. Using ultrasonic waves and light led to a 20 percent reduction in IC50, although this reduction depends on the type of nanocarriers. These findings demonstrate that smart nanoparticles' toxicity and treatment profile could be combined with light and ultrasonic waves to create a synergistic impact. The adverse effect of nanoparticles on viability can be ranked as GQD@P(VDF-TrFE) > Silibinin@P(VDF-TrFE) > HNP > Silibinin.

The results also indicate that incubation of cells with pristine Silibinin can change IC50 in the range of 150 to 400 µg/ml depending on the external factors. In comparison, IC50 varies between 70 and 180 µg/ml for the hybrid nanocarrier. Therefore, the hybrid nanoparticles not only enable us to monitor cancer cells but also it is a desired therapeutic agent carrying a lower concentration of drugs. This achievement is attributed to synergistic effects between GQDs, the drug, and the polymer. GQDs are active in producing ROS, which damages the membranes and organelles of cancer cells (Ge et al., 2014). Silibinin interferes with cancer cell function in different ways, including stimulation of apoptosis by death receptor and mitochondrial pathway and inhibition of epidermal growth factor receptor (Tiwari and Mishra, 2015). Other studies have linked the anticancer effect of Silibinin to inhibiting angiogenesis (Tiwari and Mishra, 2015). P(VDF-TrFE) enhances the release of drugs in the tumor microenvironment and supports the targeted release of therapeutic agents, creating synergy between all agents. The enhanced cytotoxicity is ascribed to the higher uptake of Silibinin and GQDs, which effectively inhibit the growth of cell lines 4T1 and MCF-7.

A comparison of the toxicity of various nanoparticles containing silibinin and GQDs with the toxicity of the nanoparticles discussed in this work can be found in Table 4 . As a general result, the IC50 of GQDs and silibinin varies depending on the carrier platforms and external factors applied.

Confocal laser scanning microscopy was employed to visualize the intracellular uptake of nanoparticles by MCF-7 cells at different incubation times (24, 48, and 72 h). Due to the red fluorescent emission of GQDs, cellular uptake can easily be traced (Fig. 8
). The nuclei were stained by DAPI (blue fluorescence). The overlay of the quantum dot’s fluorescence with the DAPI image indicates that the nanoparticles are mainly placed inside the cells. Moreover, the higher fluorescence intensity determines a more efficient uptake in the order of GQDs > GQD@P(VDF-TrFE) > HNP. The hybrid nanoparticles have a lower fluorescence intensity but can still be used for specific absorption in the target cells. Previous studies (Kersting et al., 2019; Huotari and Helenius, 2011; Maxfield and McGraw, 2004) propose that the primary accumulation of these nanoparticles is in the endosome, lysosome, and Golgi apparatus. Therefore, the mechanism of entry of these nanoparticles is mainly endocytosis.

According to the Silibinin release profiles demonstrated in Fig. 6 and the CLSM analysis shown in Fig. 8, we know that at the beginning, there are a lot of free GQDs, Silibinin, and GQD@PVDF-TrFET NPs in the culture medium. They can quickly enter cells through diffusion and uptake, resulting in a more effective anticancer impact than hybrid NPs. This was also validated by a 1-hour incubation cell apoptosis assay (Fig. 9 ). After 1-hr of incubation, the total apoptotic 4T1 cell populations that were induced by GQD-Dark (dark environment) and GQD-L + US (light and presence of ultrasound waves) were 21 % and 33 %, respectively. These percentages were slightly higher than those induced by the HNP-Dark (20 %) and HNP-L + US (26 %) groups. The only time GQDs and HNPs were added in the incubation process was at the beginning. In the meantime, concerning the HNP groups, the degradation of the HNPs resulted in an increasing amount of silibinin and GQDs being released into the medium. As a direct result of this, the cell viability of the GQD group was much lower after the presence of light and ultrasound waves in the process of cell culture than the HNP groups. Cell viability was lowest after 1-day in all groups (Fig. 9), and the total apoptotic 4T1 cell populations generated by HNP-Dark and HNP-Light + US were 66 %and 67.5 %, respectively, which were relatively lower than the GQD-Dark (70 %) and GQD-Light + US (71 %), groups. Studies have shown that nanoparticles containing the PD-1 antibody could offer a better inhibitory impact than non-targeted particles by increasing tumor cell internalization via FR-mediated endocytosis (Guo et al., 2013; Low et al., 2008). The free GQD group exhibits lower cell viability, which can be explained by the fact that free GQDs diffused rapidly into tumor cells after being added to the culture medium. Although there are different reports on the cytotoxicity of graphene quantum dots, numerous research has demonstrated that graphene quantum dots are non-toxic in the absence of light. For example, Wang et al. showed that NIH-3 T3 cells incubated with GQDs (dose: 50 μg/ml) showed a cell death rate of about 5 %, which is not visible cytotoxicity (Wang et al., 2015). In another study, the investigation of cell apoptosis revealed that GQDs (dose: 500 μg/ml) have no specific toxicity in the absence of light (Fasbender et al., 2019). Therefore, it can be concluded that the toxicity observed in this study is directly related to the presence of light, the US, and the production of reactive oxygen species by these nanoparticles.

In the next step, the Mean fluorescence intensity (MFI) of ROS generated by GQDs, GQD@PVDF-TrFE NPs, and HNPs was assessed using three fluorescent probes. Singlet oxygen is generated from the excited triplet states of photosensitizers by energy transfer called the type II mechanism (Redmond and Kochevar, 2006). In contrast, the type I mechanism generates hydroxyl radicals and superoxide anions via electron transfer (Castano et al., 2004). Under the irradiation of a Xenon lamp, the
1
O 2
generation efficiency of GQDs was greater than that of GQD@PVDF-TrFE NPs and HNPs (Fig. 10 ). The results demonstrate that the three different types of NPs produced a comparable amount of
O 2

and only GQDs are capable of photogenerated a significant amount of

O H
, whereas the other NPs only created small quantities. These three distinct categories of nanoparticles all have the capacity to generate reactive oxygen species (
1
O 2
and
O 2
· -
). Based on these findings, we concluded that the inherent optical performance of GQDs results from the photodynamic impacts. Also, other researchers have investigated the production of ROS by GQDs. For example, the production of hydroxyl radicals by GQDs (dose: 50 µg/ml) has been evaluated on MCF-7 cells, and the results are directly related to their structural defects (Wang et al., 2021). The photodynamic performance of these nanoparticles will alter depending on the coating, and the nanoparticles' toxicity can be tunable depending on whether they have a polymer or liposomal coating.

The hybrid nanoparticles were used for fluorescence imaging of a 4T1 cancer model in BALB/c mice. Fig. 11 shows the image of the tumor after IV injection of the nanoparticles at different time intervals. Since the quality of the autofluorescence signals associated with the tissue is not significant, almost no signal is recorded before the injection. When the smart nanoparticles were injected into the BALB/c mice, the fluorescence intensity in the tumor region increased with time. As a result, the tumor became distinguishable after 1 h, and the signal reached its maximum after 4 h post-injection. The injected nanoparticles in blood circulation tend to accumulate in the tumor via the blood vessels, thus providing a suitable platform for in vivo imaging (Du et al., 2017b; J. Lu et al., 2018; L. Lu et al., 2018; Marino et al., 2018).

The fluorescence imaging and biodistribution of GQDs and the smart nanoparticle were also studied after the injection of NPs into BALB/c mice. Fig. 12
a shows ex vivo images of isolated organs. The results show that the GQDs were circulated throughout the body, and were concentrated in various organs. The PL intensity of the liver, heart, kidney, and lung was significantly higher than that of the smart nanoparticles, indicating the accumulation of GQDs in these organs. Therefore, it is suggestible that the smart nanoparticles enable targeting cancer cells due to the presence of the PD-1 antibody on the surface of the liposome, which provides the opportunity for the treatment and diagnosis of breast cancer.

In vivo, PDT was investigated by using female BALB/c mice with subcutaneous 4T1 breast cancer. Three groups of tumor-bearing mice were used. In each group, seven mice were examined. The nanoparticles were IV injected into the mice at a dose of 5 mg/kg. On the first and seventh days, the mice were irradiated with a Xenon light (400–800 nm) at a power density of 80 mW cm−2 for 10 min. Besides, this group was sonicated twice, on the first and seventh day, with an ultrasound probe (frequency: 1.5 MHz, signal repetition frequency: 1.007 kHz, temporal power: 300 mW/cm2). Two control groups were used. First, the mice received the nanoparticles (5 mg/kg) but were not irradiated (the HNP group). Second, the mice that received PBS were irradiated and sonicated (the PBS group). Tumor size was measured every other day using calipers. The tumor size in mice started to decay after 17 days, and the volume of tumors was fixed at about 130 mm3 after 22 days. In contrast, tumors in the control groups grew significantly during the study (Fig. 12 b). Therefore, neither external factors (irradiation of light and ultrasound) nor injection of HNP alone inhibited the growth of tumors.

The process of treating cancer with drugs delivered by ultrasound is a complicated one. The effectiveness of treatment depends on several factors, including the type of carrier platforms, drug, dose, the US protocol, size and type of tumor, the treatment protocol, and others. In Table 5, a comparison between the hybrid nanoparticle of this work and other US-responsive nanocarriers in the literature is carried out. The therapeutic outcomes observed in vivo with these nanoparticles are also mentioned. Here, the criterion for evaluating the effectiveness of the treatment is the tumor volume inhibition rate (VIR):

The efficiency of nanoparticles with and without applying ultrasound is compared to the control group. In most cases, NP therapy results in significant tumorinhibition rates (>50 %). Nevertheless,the efficacy of therapy that included ultrasonication is consistently higher than or at least equivalent to treatment without it. For instance, Xu et al. (Xu et al., 2020) designed US-sensitive nanoparticles using mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSN) loaded with doxorubicin (DOX) and chlorin e6 (Ce6). In vitro experiments determined that MSN-DOX-Ce6 successfully limited cell proliferation under ultrasonication, although it was not significantly superior to other treatment groups. On the other hand, in vivo investigations demonstrated that MSN-DOX-Ce6 + US (VIR: 88 %) had a more effective anticancer impact than either DOX + Ce6 + US or DOX alone on xenograft tumors. All these results show that HNPs developed in this study are competitive with other nanoparticles sensitive to the US and have the ability to inhibit tumor growth and reduce its dimensions.

The mice were euthanized 24 h after treatment. The harvested tissues (lung, liver, kidney, and heart) were fixed in a neutral buffered formalin (10 %, pH = 7.26) for 48 h, and embedded in paraffin. Thin sections (5 µm) were made and stained with H & E. An independent pathologist evaluated the section with an Olympus BX51 microscope (Tokyo, Japan). Necrosis, hyperemia, hemorrhage, congestion, and acute or chronic inflammatory response were assessed in different samples. Micrographs of the tissues in the control group exhibited a normal structure without histopathological findings (Fig. 13
). Histopathological examinations of the treated groups were similar to the control group with typical structure and organization. Micrographs of the lung in dose 50 mg/kg treated BALB/c mice showed various degrees of injury. Some areas of lung sections presented the normal architecture, i.e., thin-walled alveoli surrounded by capillaries. However, acute pulmonary hemorrhage was verified by free erythrocytes (red blood cells) and edema in the alveoli. Histopathological evaluation of the liver revealed a mild infiltration of polymorphonuclear inflammatory cells, multiple necrotic foci, and hyperemia in sinusoidal spaces. Moreover, myocardial necrosis was also evident in this group. Evaluation of kidney samples showed a typical structure without any significant histopathological changes.

We developed a new liposome-based nanocarrier for photodynamic therapy using ultrasound-sensitive hybrid nanoparticles. The smart liposomal nanoparticles were composed of an anticancer drug (Silibinin), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), and a piezoelectric polymer (P(VDF-TrFE)). The nanoparticles were fabricated by the hydration thin-film method and had a narrow size distribution (230 ± 20 nm). NPs bore multi-functionalities, including red fluorescence emission, ultrasound sensitivity, good dispersibility in water, colloidal stability, and biocompatibility. The collective properties of the nanoparticles provided an appealing platform for highly efficient PDT and imaging of cancer. In vitro cytotoxicity assays and fluorescence imaging studies did not show acute toxicity or morphological changes. It was found that the cell viability in the drug-free specimens is > 90 %, even in the presence of external factors. The IC50 value range for GQD@P(VDF-TrFE), Silibinin@P(VDF-TrFE), HNP, and Silibinin was determined to be (50–100), (60–130), (70–180), and (155–400), respectively. Applying ultrasound waves and light decreased IC50 by 20 %, dependent on the type of nanocarriers. Confocal laser scanning microscopic studies determined effective cell uptake of the nanoparticles. In vivo studies also determined that the injection of smart nanoparticles and applying external stimuli (light and sonication) significantly reduced the size of 4T1 breast cancers in BALB/c mice. Ex vivo fluorescence imaging of the treated animals after 24 h intravenous injection indicated that GQDs were accumulated in the examined organs (liver, kidney, and lung). The smart nanoparticles were likely accumulated in the tumor tissue and, to a small extent, in the liver tissue. No inflammation was observed in the examined organs. The results of this study are highly encouraging as they provide considerable selectivity and evidence for the safety of smart nanoparticles for monitoring and use in cancer treatment.
